Front:
Your company needs to deploy a containerized Python application that scales based on HTTP traffic from 0 to 50 instances. The solution must support blue-green deployments and minimize operational overhead.

A) Azure Kubernetes Service with KEDA autoscaler
B) Azure Container Apps with HTTP scale rule and revision management
C) Azure Container Instances with Azure Front Door
Back: B) Azure Container Apps with HTTP scale rule and revision management
Extra: Container Apps provides built-in HTTP-based autoscaling (including scale-to-zero), native revision management for blue-green deployments, and is fully managed. AKS requires cluster management overhead. ACI doesn't natively autoscale based on HTTP traffic. Container Apps is purpose-built for microservices with minimal ops complexity.

Front:
An application must run scheduled data processing tasks every 6 hours. Each task processes 50GB of data and takes 3 hours to complete. The solution must minimize costs when idle.

A) Azure Functions with timer trigger on Consumption plan
B) Azure Container Apps with cron scale rule
C) Azure Batch with scheduled jobs
Back: B) Azure Container Apps with cron scale rule
Extra: Container Apps supports cron-based scheduling with scale-to-zero between runs and no execution time limits. Functions Consumption has 5-minute timeout (10-minute max). Batch is ideal for HPC workloads but more complex. Container Apps provides serverless containers with flexible scheduling and long-running task support.

Front:
Your organization needs to build container images from Dockerfile in a CI/CD pipeline without managing Docker hosts. Images must be scanned for vulnerabilities automatically.

A) Azure Container Registry with ACR Tasks and Microsoft Defender integration
B) Docker Hub with manual vulnerability scanning
C) Azure DevOps with self-hosted agents running Docker
Back: A) Azure Container Registry with ACR Tasks and Microsoft Defender integration
Extra: ACR Tasks builds images directly in Azure without Docker hosts (cloud-native builds), integrates with Microsoft Defender for vulnerability scanning. Docker Hub requires external service. Self-hosted agents need infrastructure management. ACR provides integrated build + security scanning in Azure-native workflow.

Front:
An application published to Azure Container Registry requires images to be automatically rebuilt when base images are updated for security patches.

A) Configure ACR Tasks with base image update triggers
B) Use GitHub Actions with scheduled builds
C) Enable Azure Security Center recommendations
Back: A) Configure ACR Tasks with base image update triggers
Extra: ACR Tasks monitors base image updates and automatically triggers rebuilds when dependencies change, ensuring containers stay patched. GitHub Actions requires manual scheduling (may miss critical patches). Security Center provides recommendations but doesn't rebuild. ACR Tasks automates base image dependency tracking and rebuilds.

Front:
Your containerized API must expose environment-specific configuration and connect to Azure SQL Database using managed identity. The container runs in Azure Container Instances.

A) Pass configuration via environment variables and use DefaultAzureCredential in SDK
B) Mount Azure Files share with configuration files
C) Store configuration in Docker image layers
Back: A) Pass configuration via environment variables and use DefaultAzureCredential in SDK
Extra: Environment variables are standard for container configuration; DefaultAzureCredential automatically uses ACI's managed identity. Mounting files adds complexity. Baking config into images violates 12-factor app principles and requires rebuilds for changes. Environment variables + managed identity is the cloud-native pattern.

Front:
A containerized microservice must be deployed with sidecar containers for logging and service mesh capabilities. The solution must support Kubernetes APIs.

A) Azure Kubernetes Service with sidecar pattern
B) Azure Container Apps with Dapr sidecars
C) Azure Container Instances with container groups
Back: B) Azure Container Apps with Dapr sidecars
Extra: Container Apps supports Kubernetes-compatible APIs with built-in Dapr sidecar support for microservices patterns. While AKS fully supports sidecars, Container Apps provides this with less operational complexity. ACI container groups support multi-container deployments but lack advanced microservices features. Container Apps balances Kubernetes compatibility with managed simplicity.

Front:
Your organization needs to pull container images from private registries during Azure Container Instances deployments. Images contain proprietary code.

A) Configure ACI with registry credentials using Azure Key Vault reference
B) Use public registry with authentication tokens
C) Enable anonymous pull access on registry
Back: A) Configure ACI with registry credentials using Azure Key Vault reference
Extra: ACI supports private registry authentication using credentials stored securely in Key Vault. Public registries expose proprietary images. Anonymous access removes security. Key Vault reference pattern secures registry credentials while allowing ACI to authenticate to private registries.

Front:
A multi-container application with web frontend and API backend must share persistent storage for uploaded files. Both containers need read-write access.

A) Azure Container Instances with Azure Files volume mount in container group
B) Azure Container Apps with multiple replicas sharing Blob Storage
C) Azure Kubernetes Service with persistent volume claims
Back: A) Azure Container Instances with Azure Files volume mount in container group
Extra: ACI container groups allow multiple containers to share volumes (Azure Files supports read-write). Container Apps replicas don't share local storage. While AKS supports PVCs, ACI container groups provide simpler multi-container shared storage for this scenario. Container groups enable sidecar patterns with shared ephemeral or persistent volumes.

Front:
Your company deploys container images tagged with Git commit SHAs. Developers need to easily identify the latest production image.

A) Use ACR repository tags with 'latest' and immutable commit SHA tags
B) Store image information in Azure Table Storage
C) Use only 'latest' tag for all deployments
Back: A) Use ACR repository tags with 'latest' and immutable commit SHA tags
Extra: ACR supports multiple tags per image; use immutable SHA tags for traceability and mutable tags like 'latest' for convenience. Table Storage is external overhead. Using only 'latest' loses version history. Multi-tagging strategy combines traceability (SHA) with convenience (latest) while maintaining immutable audit trail.

Front:
An Azure App Service application must implement health checks to remove unhealthy instances from load balancer rotation automatically.

A) Configure Health Check path in App Service settings with Load Balancing enabled
B) Use Application Insights availability tests
C) Implement custom logging with Azure Monitor alerts
Back: A) Configure Health Check path in App Service settings with Load Balancing enabled
Extra: App Service Health Check monitors endpoint and automatically removes unhealthy instances from load balancer. Availability tests monitor externally but don't trigger instance removal. Custom logging requires manual intervention. Health Check provides automatic instance health monitoring and load balancer integration.

Front:
Your App Service application logs must be retained for 90 days for compliance auditing. Logs include application traces, web server logs, and detailed error messages.

A) Enable App Service diagnostics logs with Azure Storage archive and 90-day retention policy
B) Configure Log Analytics workspace with 90-day retention
C) Use Application Insights with continuous export
Back: A) Enable App Service diagnostics logs with Azure Storage archive and 90-day retention policy
Extra: App Service diagnostics can send logs to Storage with lifecycle management policies for cost-effective long-term retention. Log Analytics has retention costs. App Insights is for telemetry not comprehensive logging. Storage-based archival with lifecycle policies optimizes costs for compliance retention requirements.

Front:
A web application on App Service must handle traffic spikes during flash sales by scaling out to 20 instances but normally runs 2 instances. Scaling must be based on CPU and HTTP queue length.

A) Configure autoscale rules with CPU percentage and HTTP queue metrics, min 2 max 20 instances
B) Manually scale before flash sales
C) Use Azure Load Balancer with static instance count
Back: A) Configure autoscale rules with CPU percentage and HTTP queue metrics, min 2 max 20 instances
Extra: App Service autoscale supports multiple metric-based rules (CPU, HTTP queue) with configurable min/max instances for automatic scaling. Manual scaling requires prediction and intervention. Static instances waste resources or underperform. Multi-metric autoscaling provides responsive, cost-effective scaling for variable workloads.

Front:
Your production App Service must be updated without downtime. Deployments need validation in production environment before receiving traffic.

A) Deploy to staging slot, test, then swap with production slot
B) Deploy directly to production with rolling updates
C) Use Traffic Manager to route between old and new deployments
Back: A) Deploy to staging slot, test, then swap with production slot
Extra: Deployment slots enable zero-downtime deployments with slot swap (instant routing switch). Staging slot runs production code before swap. Rolling updates may have partial failures. Traffic Manager adds complexity. Slot swap provides instant rollback capability and production validation before traffic shift.

Front:
An App Service application sends sensitive data to external APIs over HTTPS. Communication must use TLS 1.3 and reject older protocols for security compliance.

A) Configure TLS/SSL settings to minimum TLS version 1.3 in App Service configuration
B) Implement TLS validation in application code
C) Use Azure Application Gateway with TLS termination
Back: A) Configure TLS/SSL settings to minimum TLS version 1.3 in App Service configuration
Extra: App Service allows configuring minimum TLS version at platform level, enforcing it for all inbound connections. Code-based validation is incomplete (doesn't cover all endpoints). Application Gateway is for inbound traffic. Platform-level TLS enforcement ensures consistent security policy across application.

Front:
Your App Service application requires 8 vCPUs and 32GB RAM to run in-memory analytics. The solution must support VNet integration for database connectivity.

A) App Service Premium P3V3 plan with VNet integration
B) App Service Standard S3 with VNet integration
C) Azure Container Instances with VNet injection
Back: A) App Service Premium P3V3 plan with VNet integration
Extra: Premium P3V3 provides 8 vCPU / 32GB RAM with VNet integration support. Standard plans lack sufficient resources and VNet integration. ACI doesn't provide the application hosting features of App Service. Premium plans deliver required compute capacity with networking isolation via VNet integration.

Front:
An App Service application must connect to an on-premises SQL Server database through Azure VNet using private IP addresses only.

A) Configure VNet integration for App Service and VPN Gateway to on-premises network
B) Use public endpoint with firewall rules
C) Implement Express Route without VNet integration
Back: A) Configure VNet integration for App Service and VPN Gateway to on-premises network
Extra: VNet integration connects App Service to VNet, then VPN Gateway extends connectivity to on-premises via private IPs. Public endpoints use internet routing. ExpressRoute without VNet integration doesn't enable App Service connectivity. VNet integration + VPN Gateway provides secure private connectivity to on-premises resources.

Front:
Your App Service deployment must automatically configure app settings, connection strings, and TLS bindings during provisioning in different environments.

A) Use ARM templates or Bicep with parameterized App Service resource definitions
B) Manually configure each environment after deployment
C) Use Azure Portal with export/import settings
Back: A) Use ARM templates or Bicep with parameterized App Service resource definitions
Extra: ARM/Bicep templates enable infrastructure-as-code with parameterization for environment-specific values, ensuring consistent deployments. Manual configuration is error-prone. Portal export/import isn't automated. IaC templates provide repeatable, version-controlled, automated provisioning with environment-specific overrides.

Front:
An App Service application experiences slow performance during database queries. You need to identify the slowest operations and their dependencies automatically.

A) Enable Application Insights with instrumentation and use Application Map
B) Add custom logging for each database query
C) Use App Service diagnostics logs
Back: A) Enable Application Insights with instrumentation and use Application Map
Extra: Application Insights automatically tracks dependencies (including database calls), provides Application Map visualization, and identifies slow operations. Custom logging requires code changes and manual analysis. Diagnostic logs don't provide dependency mapping. Application Insights offers automatic APM with minimal code changes.

Front:
Your App Service must route 10% of traffic to a new version deployed in a slot for A/B testing before full rollout.

A) Configure Traffic Manager percentage routing
B) Use deployment slot traffic percentage feature
C) Implement custom routing logic in application code
Back: B) Use deployment slot traffic percentage feature
Extra: App Service slots support traffic percentage routing (e.g., 90% production, 10% staging) for gradual rollout and A/B testing. Traffic Manager operates at DNS level (slower). Code-based routing couples business logic with routing. Slot traffic routing provides platform-level traffic splitting for deployment strategies.

Front:
A Function App must process messages from an Azure Storage Queue with exactly-once processing semantics. Failed messages should be retried with exponential backoff.

A) Use Queue trigger binding with visibility timeout and built-in retry policies
B) Implement manual polling with custom retry logic
C) Use Event Grid with message forwarding
Back: A) Use Queue trigger binding with visibility timeout and built-in retry policies
Extra: Queue trigger automatically handles message visibility, deletion after successful processing, and configurable retry policies. Manual polling adds complexity. Event Grid is for events not queued messages. Queue trigger binding provides built-in exactly-once processing with automatic retry and poison message handling.

Front:
Your Function App must execute long-running workflows with human approval steps and automatic retry on transient failures, potentially running for several hours.

A) Durable Functions with orchestrator pattern and human interaction pattern
B) Timer-triggered Functions with state in Azure Table Storage
C) HTTP-triggered Functions with Azure Logic Apps
Back: A) Durable Functions with orchestrator pattern and human interaction pattern
Extra: Durable Functions provides stateful orchestrations, built-in checkpointing, automatic retries, and human interaction pattern for approval scenarios. Timer-based polling is inefficient. Logic Apps adds external dependency. Durable Functions is purpose-built for long-running stateful workflows with resilience.

Front:
An HTTP-triggered Function must return 202 Accepted immediately and provide status polling endpoints for clients to check completion of a 30-minute processing task.

A) Durable Functions with async HTTP pattern returning status query URLs
B) Synchronous function with Task.Run for background processing
C) Queue-triggered function with separate HTTP endpoint for status
Back: A) Durable Functions with async HTTP pattern returning status query URLs
Extra: Durable Functions async HTTP pattern automatically returns 202 with statusQueryGetUri, sendEventPostUri, and terminatePostUri. Task.Run blocks thread pools. Queue + status endpoint requires custom state management. Durable Functions implements async HTTP API pattern with built-in status endpoints and state persistence.

Front:
Your Function App processes blob uploads by generating thumbnails. Functions must trigger only when blobs are created in the 'uploads' container, not on updates.

A) Blob trigger binding with filter on path pattern and Event Grid integration
B) Timer trigger polling blob container
C) HTTP trigger called by Logic App
Back: A) Blob trigger binding with filter on path pattern and Event Grid integration
Extra: Blob trigger with Event Grid provides low-latency triggering with path filters (container/folder patterns). Polling adds latency and costs. HTTP trigger requires external orchestration. Blob trigger + Event Grid delivers event-driven blob processing with sub-second latency and path-based filtering.

Front:
A Function App must authenticate to Azure SQL Database without storing credentials and support local development with different authentication methods.

A) Use DefaultAzureCredential with managed identity in Azure and developer credentials locally
B) Store connection strings with passwords in local.settings.json and App Settings
C) Use Azure Key Vault SDK to retrieve passwords
Back: A) Use DefaultAzureCredential with managed identity in Azure and developer credentials locally
Extra: DefaultAzureCredential automatically uses managed identity in Azure and developer credentials (Azure CLI, Visual Studio) locally without code changes. Storing passwords violates security best practices. Key Vault still requires credential for access. DefaultAzureCredential provides environment-adaptive authentication across dev and production.

Front:
Your Function App must scale to handle 1000 concurrent requests with consistent performance. The solution must avoid cold starts during traffic spikes.

A) Premium plan with pre-warmed instances and Always Ready instances
B) Consumption plan with default settings
C) Dedicated App Service plan with manual scaling
Back: A) Premium plan with pre-warmed instances and Always Ready instances
Extra: Premium plan eliminates cold starts with Always Ready instances, supports VNet integration, and provides predictable performance. Consumption has cold starts. Dedicated requires manual scaling and wastes resources at low load. Premium balances elastic scaling with performance guarantees via pre-warmed workers.

Front:
A Function App must implement output bindings to write results to both Cosmos DB and Service Bus after processing. Multiple outputs must be atomic.

A) Configure multiple output bindings in function.json and use IAsyncCollector or return multi-output POCO
B) Use SDK clients to manually write to each service
C) Use Durable Functions activity pattern
Back: A) Configure multiple output bindings in function.json and use IAsyncCollector or return multi-output POCO
Extra: Function output bindings declaratively define outputs; use multi-output POCO or IAsyncCollector for multiple targets. SDK clients lose binding benefits (retry, connection management). Durable Functions adds complexity for simple multi-output scenario. Output bindings simplify multi-target writes with declarative configuration.

Front:
Your Function App monitoring must track custom business metrics (orders processed, revenue) alongside technical metrics (execution time, failures).

A) Use Application Insights with TrackMetric API for custom metrics and built-in telemetry
B) Write custom metrics to Azure Table Storage
C) Use function logging output only
Back: A) Use Application Insights with TrackMetric API for custom metrics and built-in telemetry
Extra: Application Insights provides built-in function telemetry plus TrackMetric/TrackEvent for custom business metrics in unified dashboard. Table Storage requires custom querying. Logs lack aggregation and alerting. Application Insights unifies technical and business metrics with powerful analytics and alerting.

Front:
A Function App must respond to changes in a Cosmos DB container and process documents in batches for efficiency, handling up to 10,000 changes per second.

A) Cosmos DB trigger with change feed and batch size configuration
B) Timer trigger polling Cosmos DB
C) Event Grid with Cosmos DB source
Back: A) Cosmos DB trigger with change feed and batch size configuration
Extra: Cosmos DB trigger uses change feed with configurable batch processing and automatic checkpointing. Polling is inefficient and misses changes. Event Grid has throughput limits. Cosmos DB trigger provides native change feed integration with scalable batch processing for high-throughput scenarios.

Front:
Your Cosmos DB container stores IoT telemetry with partition key '/deviceId'. Queries frequently filter by deviceId and timestamp range. You must minimize RU consumption.

A) Create composite index on deviceId and timestamp with proper ordering
B) Use default indexing policy
C) Create separate indexes on deviceId and timestamp individually
Back: A) Create composite index on deviceId and timestamp with proper ordering
Extra: Composite indexes optimize multi-property queries (especially range queries) by indexing combinations, reducing RU costs compared to multiple single-property indexes. Default policy indexes everything (may be inefficient). Separate indexes don't optimize combined queries. Composite indexes provide query-specific optimization for lowest RU consumption.

Front:
An application queries Cosmos DB for product catalog data read by multiple regions. Data is updated daily in the primary region. The solution must minimize read latency globally and costs.

A) Multi-region writes with eventual consistency
B) Multi-region reads with session consistency and primary region writes
C) Single region with aggressive query caching
Back: B) Multi-region reads with session consistency and primary region writes
Extra: Multi-region reads replicate data to regions close to users (low read latency); single-region write reduces costs (no write conflicts). Session consistency balances performance and freshness. Multi-region writes increase costs without benefit for read-heavy workloads. Regional read replicas optimize global read performance while controlling write costs.

Front:
Your Cosmos DB container must guarantee that operations read the most recent committed data in the primary region, even during replication lag to secondary regions.

A) Strong consistency level
B) Bounded staleness consistency
C) Session consistency
Back: A) Strong consistency level
Extra: Strong consistency guarantees reads return latest committed write across all regions, at cost of higher latency. Bounded staleness allows configurable lag. Session consistency guarantees only within session. Strong consistency ensures absolute data freshness when required for business logic (financial transactions, inventory).

Front:
An application stores user profiles in Cosmos DB with partition key '/userId'. Operations must update a user's document atomically with ACID guarantees without affecting other users.

A) Use TransactionalBatch for operations within same logical partition
B) Use stored procedure for cross-partition transactions
C) Implement optimistic concurrency with ETags
Back: A) Use TransactionalBatch for operations within same logical partition
Extra: TransactionalBatch provides ACID guarantees for operations within the same logical partition (userId). Stored procedures can't span partitions. ETags prevent conflicts but don't provide multi-operation atomicity. TransactionalBatch delivers true ACID transactions for multi-document operations within partition boundaries.

Front:
Your Cosmos DB workload processes real-time data streams and needs to react to document changes, forwarding them to Azure Event Hubs for downstream processing.

A) Use Change Feed Processor to monitor changes and send to Event Hubs
B) Use Cosmos DB trigger in Azure Function
C) Poll Cosmos DB periodically with timestamp queries
Back: A) Use Change Feed Processor to monitor changes and send to Event Hubs
Extra: Change Feed Processor provides reliable, scalable change capture with lease management and checkpointing, ideal for high-throughput streaming scenarios. Functions Cosmos trigger is simpler but adds function overhead. Polling is inefficient and may miss changes. Change Feed Processor is purpose-built for continuous change data capture at scale.

Front:
An application must load a 5MB user profile document and filter client-side properties. Cosmos DB queries should minimize RU consumption.

A) Use SQL query with SELECT to project only required properties
B) Query the full document and filter in application code
C) Use ReadItem with full document retrieval
Back: A) Use SQL query with SELECT to project only required properties
Extra: Projecting specific properties with SELECT reduces data transfer and RU consumption (charges based on response size). Full document retrieval wastes RUs and bandwidth. ReadItem is for point reads (single document by ID/partition key). Query projection optimizes RU costs by returning only necessary data.

Front:
Your Cosmos DB container must implement time-to-live (TTL) to automatically delete expired session data after 24 hours while retaining user profile documents indefinitely.

A) Enable container-level TTL with DefaultTimeToLive and override per-document with ttl property
B) Use Azure Function timer trigger to delete expired documents
C) Implement application logic to delete old documents
Back: A) Enable container-level TTL with DefaultTimeToLive and override per-document with ttl property
Extra: Cosmos DB TTL automatically expires documents without RU cost. Set DefaultTimeToLive at container level, override with per-document ttl property (set to -1 for no expiration). Timer-based deletion consumes RUs and adds complexity. TTL is declarative, automatic, and RU-free for document lifecycle management.

Front:
An application requires consistent read-your-writes guarantee within user sessions but can tolerate eventual consistency for reads from other users.

A) Session consistency level
B) Strong consistency level
C) Eventual consistency with custom logic
Back: A) Session consistency level
Extra: Session consistency provides read-your-writes guarantee within session (user sees their updates immediately) with lower latency than strong consistency. Strong adds unnecessary latency for cross-user reads. Eventual lacks within-session guarantees. Session consistency balances user experience (consistency within session) and performance.

Front:
Your Cosmos DB solution must distribute write traffic across multiple partition keys to avoid hot partitions. Documents are orders with incrementing order IDs.

A) Use composite partition key or hash of order ID to distribute load
B) Use order ID directly as partition key
C) Use customer ID as partition key
Back: A) Use composite partition key or hash of order ID to distribute load
Extra: Sequential order IDs create hot partition (all writes to single partition). Hashing distributes writes across partitions. Customer ID may also create hot partitions (high-value customers). Composite keys or hash-based partition keys distribute writes uniformly, preventing throughput bottlenecks from hot partitions.

Front:
An application uploads user profile images (200KB each) with metadata. Images must be accessible via HTTPS URLs and metadata queryable separately.

A) Store images in Blob Storage with metadata, reference blob URL in database
B) Store images as base64 in SQL Database with metadata
C) Store images and metadata in Cosmos DB documents
Back: A) Store images in Blob Storage with metadata, reference blob URL in database
Extra: Blob Storage is optimized for binary files with HTTP access and custom metadata support. Storing binary data in databases increases costs and reduces query performance. Store blobs in Blob Storage, metadata in database with URL reference. This pattern separates file storage from metadata for optimal performance and cost.

Front:
Your application must upload 2GB video files to Blob Storage with resumable uploads to handle network interruptions without restarting.

A) Use BlockBlob with staged blocks and commit block list
B) Use PutBlob operation for entire file
C) Use PageBlob with page updates
Back: A) Use BlockBlob with staged blocks and commit block list
Extra: Block blobs support uploading files in blocks (up to 50,000 blocks of 4000 MiB each), enabling parallel and resumable uploads. PutBlob has size limits and no resume capability. Page blobs are for random read/write (VHDs). Block blob staging provides resilient upload for large files with network reliability.

Front:
A web application serves images stored in Blob Storage. Users report slow load times from distant geographic regions.

A) Enable Azure CDN with blob storage endpoint as origin
B) Replicate blobs to multiple storage accounts manually
C) Use read-access geo-redundant storage (RA-GRS)
Back: A) Enable Azure CDN with blob storage endpoint as origin
Extra: Azure CDN caches blob content at edge locations globally, reducing latency for distant users. Manual replication adds management overhead. RA-GRS provides disaster recovery not performance. CDN delivers low-latency content distribution via edge caching for globally distributed users.

Front:
Your application must automatically delete blob versions older than 90 days and move current blobs to Cool tier after 30 days of no access to reduce costs.

A) Configure lifecycle management policy with version delete and tier transition rules
B) Use Azure Function with timer trigger to manage blobs
C) Manually archive blobs periodically
Back: A) Configure lifecycle management policy with version delete and tier transition rules
Extra: Lifecycle management policies automate blob tiering and deletion based on age or access patterns without code. Function-based management consumes compute and requires maintenance. Manual processes are error-prone. Lifecycle policies provide declarative, cost-effective blob lifecycle automation.

Front:
An application must ensure blob uploads succeed only if the blob has not been modified by another client since last read (optimistic concurrency).

A) Use ETag with If-Match header in upload request
B) Use blob leases for exclusive access
C) Implement custom locking in database
Back: A) Use ETag with If-Match header in upload request
Extra: ETags provide version tokens; If-Match header ensures operations succeed only if ETag matches (no concurrent modification). Leases provide pessimistic locking (not optimistic). Database locking adds external dependency. ETag-based conditional requests implement optimistic concurrency natively in Blob Storage.

Front:
Your application must generate time-limited URLs allowing temporary read access to private blobs without exposing storage account keys.

A) Generate Shared Access Signature (SAS) token with read permission and expiration
B) Change blob access level to public temporarily
C) Provide storage account key to clients
Back: A) Generate Shared Access Signature (SAS) token with read permission and expiration
Extra: SAS tokens grant granular, time-limited access to specific blobs without exposing account keys. Public access removes security. Sharing account keys is dangerous. SAS provides fine-grained, time-bound delegated access with revocability via stored access policies.

Front:
An application must read blobs even during primary region outage. Data can be eventually consistent across regions.

A) Use RA-GRS (read-access geo-redundant storage) with secondary endpoint
B) Use LRS (locally redundant storage) with application-level replication
C) Use ZRS (zone-redundant storage)
Back: A) Use RA-GRS (read-access geo-redundant storage) with secondary endpoint
Extra: RA-GRS replicates data to paired region with read access to secondary endpoint during primary outage. LRS is single-region only. ZRS is within single region. RA-GRS provides geo-redundancy with read access to secondary replica for disaster recovery scenarios.

Front:
Your application processes blob uploads with metadata tags for classification. Queries must efficiently retrieve blobs by tag values without listing all blobs.

A) Use blob index tags with FindBlobsByTags query
B) Store metadata in blob properties and enumerate blobs
C) Maintain separate database index of blobs
Back: A) Use blob index tags with FindBlobsByTags query
Extra: Blob index tags enable efficient tag-based queries across storage account without enumeration. Blob metadata requires listing all blobs. External database adds consistency overhead. Blob index tags provide queryable metadata directly in Blob Storage for efficient filtering and searching.

Front:
An application uploads critical financial documents to blob storage. Modifications and deletions must be prevented for 7 years for regulatory compliance.

A) Configure time-based retention policy with legal hold on container
B) Use blob soft delete with 7-year retention
C) Remove write permissions in IAM roles
Back: A) Configure time-based retention policy with legal hold on container
Extra: Time-based retention policies enforce WORM (write once, read many) semantics, preventing modification/deletion for specified period. Soft delete allows eventual deletion. IAM doesn't enforce immutability. Immutable storage with retention policies provides regulatory-compliant data protection against all modification including administrator actions.

Front:
Your web application must enable users to authenticate using their existing Google and Facebook accounts without managing credentials.

A) Configure Azure AD B2C with social identity provider federation
B) Implement OAuth flow manually in application code
C) Use Azure AD with guest accounts
Back: A) Configure Azure AD B2C with social identity provider federation
Extra: Azure AD B2C provides built-in social identity provider integration (Google, Facebook, etc.) with user management and flows. Manual OAuth adds complexity and security risks. Azure AD guest accounts are for B2B scenarios not consumer social login. B2C is purpose-built for consumer identity with social federation.

Front:
An Azure Function must authenticate to an Azure Storage account without credentials in code. The solution must support automatic key rotation.

A) Configure managed identity for Function App and grant RBAC role on storage account
B) Store connection string in Key Vault and retrieve at runtime
C) Use storage account key in application settings
Back: A) Configure managed identity for Function App and grant RBAC role on storage account
Extra: Managed identity eliminates credentials entirely; Azure AD provides tokens automatically. Key Vault still involves secrets. Account keys require manual rotation. Managed identity + RBAC delivers credential-free authentication with automatic token rotation and lifecycle management.

Front:
Your API must validate JWT tokens from Azure AD, require 'api://myapi/read' scope claim, and reject expired or tampered tokens.

A) Use JWT Bearer authentication middleware validating issuer, audience, and claims
B) Manually parse JWT and verify signature
C) Accept tokens without validation
Back: A) Use JWT Bearer authentication middleware validating issuer, audience, and claims
Extra: JWT Bearer middleware validates signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and claims declaratively. Manual validation is error-prone (crypto complexity). No validation is insecure. Middleware-based JWT validation provides robust, standardized token verification with claims-based authorization.

Front:
An application requires access to Azure Key Vault secrets. Authentication must work seamlessly in Azure environments and local development without code changes.

A) Use DefaultAzureCredential which chains managed identity, Azure CLI, and Visual Studio credentials
B) Use separate credential types with environment-specific code
C) Store credentials in environment variables
Back: A) Use DefaultAzureCredential which chains managed identity, Azure CLI, and Visual Studio credentials
Extra: DefaultAzureCredential tries multiple credential sources in order (managed identity in Azure, developer credentials locally) without code changes. Environment-specific code increases complexity. Environment variables expose credentials. DefaultAzureCredential provides environment-adaptive authentication with unified API.

Front:
Your multi-tenant SaaS application must isolate customer data in separate databases. Authentication should use customer-specific Azure AD tenants.

A) Implement Azure AD multi-tenant application registration with tenant ID claim routing
B) Use single-tenant Azure AD with guest users
C) Implement custom authentication per customer
Back: A) Implement Azure AD multi-tenant application registration with tenant ID claim routing
Extra: Multi-tenant Azure AD app registration allows users from any Azure AD tenant to sign in; use tenant ID claim to route to customer-specific resources. Guest users don't scale for SaaS. Custom auth lacks Azure AD integration benefits. Multi-tenant app registration enables SaaS authentication with tenant isolation.

Front:
An application must retrieve database password from Key Vault, caching it for 1 hour to reduce Key Vault calls and costs.

A) Use Key Vault SDK with in-memory cache and 1-hour TTL
B) Store secret in variable on first retrieval without expiration
C) Query Key Vault on every database connection
Back: A) Use Key Vault SDK with in-memory cache and 1-hour TTL
Extra: Caching secrets balances security and performance; TTL ensures eventual password rotation. Permanent caching ignores rotation. Per-connection queries increase costs and latency. Time-limited caching optimizes Key Vault costs while supporting secret rotation operations.

Front:
Your application uses certificates for mutual TLS authentication. Certificates must be stored securely and rotated automatically every 90 days.

A) Store certificates in Key Vault with auto-rotation policy configured with certificate issuer
B) Store certificate files in Blob Storage with manual rotation
C) Embed certificates in application code
Back: A) Store certificates in Key Vault with auto-rotation policy configured with certificate issuer
Extra: Key Vault stores certificates as managed secrets with auto-renewal via integrated issuers (e.g., DigiCert, Let's Encrypt). Blob Storage lacks rotation features. Embedded certificates require redeployment. Key Vault provides centralized certificate lifecycle management with automatic renewal.

Front:
An Azure service principal must access Key Vault secrets. Access must follow principle of least privilege with auditing.

A) Grant Key Vault Secret User RBAC role and enable diagnostic logs to Log Analytics
B) Use access policies with Get/List permissions
C) Share Key Vault contributor role
Back: A) Grant Key Vault Secret User RBAC role and enable diagnostic logs to Log Analytics
Extra: RBAC provides granular permissions (Secret User grants read-only secret access). Diagnostic logs audit all operations. Access policies are legacy (RBAC is recommended). Contributor grants excessive permissions. RBAC + audit logging implements least privilege with security monitoring.

Front:
Your application must implement database connection strings with different values for development, staging, and production environments.

A) Use Azure App Configuration with labels for environment-specific values
B) Use separate Key Vaults per environment
C) Hard-code connection strings per environment in application
Back: A) Use Azure App Configuration with labels for environment-specific values
Extra: App Configuration labels enable environment-specific values for same key (e.g., 'Production', 'Development' labels). Multiple Key Vaults add management overhead. Hard-coding violates configuration management best practices. App Configuration with labels provides centralized, environment-specific configuration management.

Front:
An application monitors performance metrics and you observe 95th percentile request latency increased from 100ms to 500ms. Most requests remain fast. You need to identify the slow operations.

A) Use Application Insights with Performance blade and transaction search filtering by duration
B) Check average response time in Azure Monitor metrics
C) Review application logs for errors
Back: A) Use Application Insights with Performance blade and transaction search filtering by duration
Extra: Application Insights tracks every request telemetry; Performance blade shows percentile distributions and transaction search enables filtering outliers. Average metrics hide outliers. Logs don't provide performance distribution analysis. Application Insights provides detailed performance diagnostics with outlier identification.

Front:
Your production application experiences intermittent errors. You need to correlate failures across multiple components (web API, database, external services) to identify root cause.

A) Use Application Insights distributed tracing and Application Map
B) Manually correlate timestamps across separate logs
C) Increase logging verbosity in application
Back: A) Use Application Insights distributed tracing and Application Map
Extra: Application Insights automatically tracks operations across components with correlation IDs, visualizing dependencies in Application Map. Manual correlation is time-consuming. Verbose logging adds noise. Distributed tracing provides automatic end-to-end request tracking across distributed system components.

Front:
Your API must alert the operations team when error rate exceeds 5% over 5-minute window or average response time exceeds 2 seconds.

A) Create Application Insights metric alert with dynamic threshold and action group
B) Write custom code to check metrics and send emails
C) Manually monitor dashboard during business hours
Back: A) Create Application Insights metric alert with dynamic threshold and action group
Extra: Application Insights metric alerts automatically evaluate conditions and trigger action groups (email, SMS, webhook). Custom code adds overhead and reliability concerns. Manual monitoring doesn't scale. Metric alerts provide automated, configurable anomaly detection with multi-channel notifications.

Front:
An Azure Function processes queue messages. Some messages cause unhandled exceptions. You need to automatically capture exception details for debugging.

A) Application Insights automatically captures unhandled exceptions with stack traces
B) Wrap all function code in try-catch and log manually
C) Enable function app diagnostics logs
Back: A) Application Insights automatically captures unhandled exceptions with stack traces
Extra: Application Insights integrates with Azure Functions to automatically capture exceptions, including stack traces, custom properties, and dependencies. Try-catch adds boilerplate. Diagnostics logs lack structured exception tracking. Application Insights provides automatic exception tracking with rich context.

Front:
Your web application must track custom business events like 'OrderCompleted' with properties (orderValue, customerId) for analytics.

A) Use TrackEvent API in Application Insights with custom properties dictionary
B) Write events to Azure Table Storage
C) Add custom dimensions to existing telemetry only
Back: A) Use TrackEvent API in Application Insights with custom properties dictionary
Extra: TrackEvent sends custom events with properties to Application Insights for analytics, querying, and visualization. Table Storage requires custom analysis pipeline. Attaching to existing telemetry loses event semantics. TrackEvent provides structured custom telemetry with Application Insights analytics integration.

Front:
An application experiences performance degradation in production. You need to understand which code paths consume the most time.

A) Enable Application Insights Profiler for production performance analysis
B) Add manual timing code throughout application
C) Use Visual Studio debugger in production
Back: A) Enable Application Insights Profiler for production performance analysis
Extra: Application Insights Profiler captures production performance traces showing method-level timing without code changes. Manual timing adds overhead and clutter. Production debugging is dangerous and not scalable. Profiler provides low-overhead production profiling with detailed call stack analysis.

Front:
Your API must implement sampling to reduce Application Insights telemetry volume and costs while preserving statistical accuracy.

A) Configure adaptive sampling in Application Insights SDK settings
B) Manually track only 10% of requests in code
C) Disable telemetry for GET requests
Back: A) Configure adaptive sampling in Application Insights SDK settings
Extra: Adaptive sampling automatically adjusts sample rate to maintain target volume while preserving statistical properties and correlated telemetry. Manual sampling loses correlation. Disabling telemetry creates blind spots. Adaptive sampling intelligently reduces volume while maintaining diagnostic capability.

Front:
Your application must monitor upstream API availability from multiple global regions and alert on failures.

A) Configure Application Insights availability tests from multiple locations
B) Implement health check endpoint polled by Azure Function
C) Use Azure Service Health notifications
Back: A) Configure Application Insights availability tests from multiple locations
Extra: Application Insights availability tests (URL ping, multi-step) run from distributed Azure locations with automatic alerting. Function-based polling is single-region and requires custom logic. Service Health monitors Azure platform not custom apps. Availability tests provide global synthetic monitoring with alerting.

Front:
Your web application must implement authentication and rate limiting for external API consumption while providing API key management for clients.

A) Azure API Management with subscription key authentication and rate-limit policy
B) Implement rate limiting in application code
C) Use Azure Front Door with WAF rules
Back: A) Azure API Management with subscription key authentication and rate-limit policy
Extra: API Management provides subscription keys, rate limiting policies, quotas, and client portal for key management. Code-based rate limiting lacks centralized management. Front Door WAF lacks API-specific features. API Management delivers comprehensive API gateway capabilities with declarative policies.

Front:
An API Gateway must validate structure of incoming JSON request payloads before forwarding to backend services to prevent invalid requests.

A) Use API Management validate-content policy with JSON schema
B) Implement validation logic in each backend service
C) Use Azure Function proxy with custom validation
Back: A) Use API Management validate-content policy with JSON schema
Extra: API Management validate-content policy enforces JSON schema validation at gateway, rejecting invalid requests before backend. Backend validation doesn't prevent unnecessary calls. Function proxies add latency and complexity. Gateway-level validation protects backends and enforces API contracts centrally.

Front:
Your API must transform XML responses from legacy backend services to JSON for modern client applications.

A) Use API Management with xml-to-json policy in outbound section
B) Implement transformation in client applications
C) Create transformation service middleware
Back: A) Use API Management with xml-to-json policy in outbound section
Extra: API Management transformation policies (xml-to-json, json-to-xml) enable declarative format transformation at gateway. Client-side transformation increases client complexity. Middleware service adds latency. Gateway transformation provides protocol mediation without backend or client changes.

Front:
An API must restrict certain operations to internal network clients only while allowing public access to other operations.

A) Use API Management with ip-filter policy on specific operations or APIs
B) Implement IP filtering in backend service
C) Use network security groups on API Management subnet
Back: A) Use API Management with ip-filter policy on specific operations or APIs
Extra: API Management ip-filter policy restricts access at operation level based on client IP. Backend filtering loses centralized control. NSGs apply to entire APIM instance not specific operations. Operation-level IP filtering enables granular access control per API endpoint.

Front:
Your API Management instance must route requests to different backend services based on URL path segments.

A) Configure API Management operations with different backend-service policies
B) Use application code to route requests
C) Deploy multiple API Management instances
Back: A) Configure API Management operations with different backend-service policies
Extra: API Management operations can have unique backend policies, enabling path-based routing (e.g., /api/v1/* → backend1, /api/v2/* → backend2). Application code couples routing with business logic. Multiple APIM instances increase costs. Operation-level backend configuration provides flexible request routing.

Front:
An application must receive notifications within seconds when new blobs are uploaded to Azure Storage across multiple storage accounts.

A) Use Azure Event Grid with blob storage event subscription and webhook/Azure Function handler
B) Use blob trigger polling storage accounts
C) Implement timer-based blob listing
Back: A) Use Azure Event Grid with blob storage event subscription and webhook/Azure Function handler
Extra: Event Grid delivers blob events (creation, deletion) within seconds via push notification. Blob trigger has polling delay. Timer-based listing is inefficient and misses rapid changes. Event Grid provides reactive event-driven architecture with sub-second latency for storage events.

Front:
Your IoT solution must ingest 100,000+ events per second from devices, process them in order per device, and store for analytics.

A) Use Azure Event Hubs with partition key on device ID and Stream Analytics or Functions
B) Use Azure Service Bus queues
C) Use Azure Event Grid with multiple subscriptions
Back: A) Use Azure Event Hubs with partition key on device ID and Stream Analytics or Functions
Extra: Event Hubs is purpose-built for high-throughput event ingestion with partitioning for ordering per key (device). Service Bus is for messaging not telemetry at scale. Event Grid lacks throughput capacity for 100K+ events/sec. Event Hubs provides massive-scale event ingestion with ordering and retention.

Front:
An application must capture Event Hub stream events, batch them by time window (5 minutes), and store to Blob Storage automatically.

A) Use Event Hubs Capture feature with time-based windowing to Blob Storage
B) Use Azure Function with Event Hub trigger and manual batching
C) Use Logic App with scheduled polling
Back: A) Use Event Hubs Capture feature with time-based windowing to Blob Storage
Extra: Event Hubs Capture automatically batches and stores events to Blob Storage or Data Lake based on time/size windows without code. Function-based capture requires manual batching logic. Logic Apps poll (not real-time). Capture provides zero-code event archival with windowing configuration.

Front:
Your event-driven architecture must guarantee message delivery with retry logic and dead-letter queue for processing failures.

A) Use Azure Service Bus queues with MaxDeliveryCount and dead-letter queue
B) Use Azure Event Grid with retry policy
C) Use Azure Storage Queues with manual retry code
Back: A) Use Azure Service Bus queues with MaxDeliveryCount and dead-letter queue
Extra: Service Bus provides enterprise messaging with built-in retry policies, MaxDeliveryCount, and automatic dead-letter queue for poison messages. Event Grid has limited retry (24 hours). Storage Queues lack dead-letter features. Service Bus delivers reliable messaging with comprehensive failure handling.

Front:
An application publishes order events that multiple downstream services must process independently. Each service must receive all events.

A) Use Azure Service Bus topic with multiple subscriptions (one per service)
B) Use Azure Service Bus queue with competing consumers
C) Use Azure Event Hubs with multiple consumer groups
Back: A) Use Azure Service Bus topic with multiple subscriptions (one per service)
Extra: Service Bus topics with subscriptions enable pub/sub pattern where each subscription receives message copy. Queues distribute messages (competing consumers not broadcast). Event Hubs is for telemetry not enterprise messaging. Topics provide message fan-out for independent concurrent processing.

Front:
Your messaging solution must implement deferred message processing where messages are received but processing is delayed until a specific condition is met.

A) Use Azure Service Bus with message deferral and sequence number-based retrieval
B) Use Azure Storage Queue with visibility timeout
C) Use Azure Event Grid with scheduled delivery
Back: A) Use Azure Service Bus with message deferral and sequence number-based retrieval
Extra: Service Bus supports message deferral (park message with sequence number, retrieve later) for sophisticated workflows. Storage Queue visibility timeout is fixed delay not conditional. Event Grid lacks deferral. Service Bus message deferral enables complex message processing patterns with state management.

Front:
An application sends messages to Service Bus queue. Processing requires exactly-once delivery semantics even during transient failures.

A) Service Bus sessions with PeekLock receive mode and Complete operation
B) ReceiveAndDelete mode for automatic completion
C) Manual message deletion after processing
Back: A) Service Bus sessions with PeekLock receive mode and Complete operation
Extra: PeekLock mode locks message during processing; Complete operation confirms success (message deleted), automatic retry on failure. ReceiveAndDelete lacks failure recovery. Manual deletion adds complexity. PeekLock + Complete implements exactly-once processing with automatic retry and idempotency.

Front:
Your microservices architecture requires asynchronous communication with lightweight messages containing only change notifications (event carried state transfer).

A) Use Azure Event Grid for lightweight event notifications with custom event schema
B) Use Azure Service Bus with large message payloads
C) Use Azure Event Hubs for event streaming
Back: A) Use Azure Event Grid for lightweight event notifications with custom event schema
Extra: Event Grid is optimized for lightweight discrete events (notifications) with pub/sub routing. Service Bus is for complex messaging workflows. Event Hubs is for high-throughput streams. Event Grid provides reactive event distribution for loosely coupled microservices communication.

Front:
An application must process messages from Storage Queue with automatic retry on transient errors and scale based on queue depth.

A) Use Azure Function with Queue trigger and built-in retry policy and KEDA scaling
B) Implement worker role with manual polling and threading
C) Use Logic App with recurrence trigger
Back: A) Use Azure Function with Queue trigger and built-in retry policy and KEDA scaling
Extra: Queue trigger automatically polls, processes, and scales based on queue depth with built-in retry. Worker roles require infrastructure management. Logic Apps scheduled polling adds latency. Queue trigger provides serverless queue processing with automatic scaling and retry.

Front:
Your event handler must process Event Grid events with custom domain validation during subscription creation.

A) Implement webhook endpoint that responds to SubscriptionValidation event with validation code
B) Use manual validation in Azure Portal
C) Disable validation for faster setup
Back: A) Implement webhook endpoint that responds to SubscriptionValidation event with validation code
Extra: Event Grid requires webhook validation (respond with validation code) to prove endpoint ownership. Manual validation isn't automated. Disabling validation isn't possible. Webhook validation ensures event subscriptions are created only by authorized endpoint owners.

Front:
An application must filter Service Bus topic messages before delivery to subscriptions based on message properties.

A) Configure subscription filters with SQL-like expressions on message properties
B) Implement filtering logic in message consumers
C) Create separate topics per message type
Back: A) Configure subscription filters with SQL-like expressions on message properties
Extra: Service Bus subscription filters evaluate message properties server-side, delivering only matching messages. Consumer filtering wastes bandwidth. Multiple topics add management overhead. Subscription filters provide declarative server-side message routing without consumer logic.
